headline: Cat Wu on shipping speed: how Anthropic compresses six-month timelines to a week
digest_md:
Cat Wu, Head of Product for Claude Code, argues the PM role is being rewritten in real time by AI-native engineering velocity. Six-month feature timelines have dropped to one week — sometimes one day. The traditional PM toolkit (multi-quarter roadmap alignment, partner-team coordination, detailed PRDs) is now the bottleneck, not the cure.

Her response is twofold: build *concept corners* — small product surfaces where one engineer can ship end-to-end weekly without coordination overhead — and hire engineers with strong product taste rather than additional PMs to do the speccing for them.

The argument cuts across role definitions. *All the roles are merging,* Wu says. PMs do engineering work; engineers do PM work; designers PM and ship code. On her team, the highest-leverage hire isn't another PM — it's an engineer with product taste, capable of going from user feedback on Twitter to a shipped product by Friday with almost no PM involvement.

That has implications for what a PM should actually *do* all day. Wu's framing: the PM's question is no longer how to align quarterly roadmaps. It's how to maximize what today's model already does well, before it gets superseded.
takeaways: Build for the model you have, not the model you'll have. Six-week plans beat nine-month roadmaps.
If your team can't start work without a detailed PRD from you, you are the bottleneck.
Product taste is built by using products, not reading frameworks. One hour a day, every day.

source.title: How Anthropic's product team moves faster than anyone else

questions.scenario_md:
You're a PM at a 100-person AI startup. You've spent the last month preparing a 9-month roadmap built around the model capabilities your team *expects* to ship by Q3. Cat Wu argues PMs should be the right amount of AGI-pilled — neither under nor over.

Tomorrow morning, the most aligned move is —

options.text: Add contingency caveats to each 9-month milestone.
Scrap the 9-month plan; replace it with a 6-week plan that maximizes what today's model already does well, shipping iteratively.
Deliver the 9-month plan as scoped — long-term planning is your job.
Deliver the plan to leadership but tell engineering to ignore it.

questions.explanation_md: Cat Wu argues PMs should be the right amount of AGI-pilled — building for the model you have, not the model you'll have. Future-proofing roadmaps assumes a level of model stability that AI-native products don't have.
questions.pm_takeaway: This week: scrap your forward-looking roadmap. Plan only what today's model can already do well.

citation.title: How Anthropic's product team moves faster than anyone else
citation.quote_excerpt: I think it is very hard to be the right amount of AGI-pilled. It's very easy to build the product for the super AGI strong model. The hard thing is figuring out for the current model, how do you elicit the maximum capability?

questions.scenario_md:
You're a B2B PM, 6 years in, well-respected. You write thorough PRDs, run aligned sprint planning, and own stakeholder communication. You're reading more about AI-native PMs and starting to wonder whether your toolkit is becoming the bottleneck instead of the lever.

Per Cat Wu's framing, the most concerning warning sign in *your own* current work is —

options.text: Your team won't start work until you've written a detailed PRD.
Your stakeholders push back on aggressive timelines.
Your engineers don't have AI-augmented dev tools yet.
Your team has several half-finished features in flight.

questions.explanation_md: Cat Wu's diagnosis is that PM rigor (detailed PRDs, alignment, research cycles) is itself a barrier to shipping speed in AI-native products. If work in your team can't start without you, you're the bottleneck — not engineering capacity.
questions.pm_takeaway: If your team can't start work without you, you're the bottleneck. The fix isn't to write PRDs faster — it's to make the PRD optional.

citation.title: How Anthropic's product team moves faster than anyone else
citation.quote_excerpt: You're interviewing hundreds of PMs and you just keep feeling like they're approaching it very incorrectly.

questions.scenario_md:
You want to develop "product taste" as Cat Wu describes it. You have one hour a day outside your normal work that you can invest in it.

The most leveraged use of that hour is —

options.text: Read PM books and frameworks to deepen your strategic toolkit.
Take a structured online course on AI product management.
Run additional weekly user interviews to understand customer needs.
Use 5–10 AI products yourself daily, taking notes on what works, what feels broken, and why.

questions.explanation_md: Product taste isn't a framework you learn; it's a sense you build by using products critically every day. Cat Wu's framing: rare skill, built through repeated exposure to good and bad product decisions.
questions.pm_takeaway: This week: pick 5 AI products you've never used. Spend 10 minutes in each, every day. Note one thing each does well and one thing it gets wrong.

citation.title: How Anthropic's product team moves faster than anyone else
citation.quote_excerpt: I think product taste is still a very rare skill to have, and we'll pretty much hire anyone who we feel has demonstrated this strongly.

questions.scenario_md:
Looking at your last week as a PM: mornings in stakeholder syncs, midday writing PRDs and updating roadmap docs, afternoons in 1:1s with engineering and design. You feel productive.

Per Cat Wu's framing, the element most clearly missing from your week is —

options.text: More 1:1s with cross-functional partners to align faster.
Time *in* the product itself — using it as a user, noting friction, building intuition for what should change.
Structured PRD reviews to reduce engineering rework.
A weekly retrospective with engineering on velocity.

questions.explanation_md: Cat Wu's barrier-removal framework starts with self-awareness: if your calendar is full of coordination, your *product* is invisible to you. More coordination is the opposite direction; the missing element is direct product exposure.
questions.pm_takeaway: If you can't recall the last time you used your own product as a user this week, you're operating on second-hand information. That's where the bottleneck starts.

citation.title: How Anthropic's product team moves faster than anyone else
citation.quote_excerpt: We want to remove every single barrier to shipping things. The timelines for a lot of our product features have gone down from six month to one month and sometimes to even one day.

questions.scenario_md:
Cat Wu describes engineers going from "user feedback on Twitter to shipped product by week's end with almost no product involvement." You're at a 300-person fintech with Slack-based user feedback, mandatory code review SLAs, and a 2-week QA cycle.

The closest viable equivalent is —

options.text: Rotate engineers weekly to monitor Slack for feedback.
Move feedback handling to a dedicated triage team so engineers focus on coding.
Pick one engineer to own one feedback-driven feature end-to-end (triage → design → code → ship), within the existing review/QA cycle.
Eliminate code review and QA so engineers can ship instantly.

questions.explanation_md: Cat Wu's framework adapts to constrained contexts by finding a *surface* where end-to-end shipping is possible, not by removing the constraint everywhere. Triage-to-ship by one engineer within existing safeguards is the closest viable equivalent.
questions.pm_takeaway: You don't need to break your whole org. Find one surface where you can model the new way this week.

citation.title: How Anthropic's product team moves faster than anyone else
citation.quote_excerpt: There are many engineers on our team who are fully able to end to end go from see user feedback on Twitter through to ship a product at the end of the week with almost no product involvement.
